title: A fireside chat - Daphne Koller - RECOMB/RSG 2018
text:
okay it's it's a great honor and
pleasure to introduce to you our first
keynote speaker Daphne Koller Daphne is
the founder and CEO of an interesting
new company called in seat row you can
imagine how they came up with that name
but she will tell us later in seat row
and where they are trying to do drug
development using you know all the
experience that Daphne has in machine
learning and artificial intelligence but
this is only her last week she had
previous gigs one of which is she was a
professor of computer science at
Stanford where she did fantastic work in
applying graphical models to
computational biology among other things
and she she when she was you know at the
top of the mountain she she said well
I'm going to create a new company and
she created co-founded Coursera and I
think everybody here probably took a
course or or or knows of it apparently
they are like more than 30 million
people who took courses from Coursera so
we really reached everybody in the field
and and and a her CV is plentiful in
Awards the one that I like the most is
the MacArthur Fellowship that is called
the genius award that that is only
awarded to really people who have great
creativity and good ideas energies she
she is very important influential and
creative as I don't say that right and
Newsweek said that she was amongst the
10 more important people in 2010 Time
magazine's the hundred most influential
people in 2012 and Fast Company the most
creative people in 2014 so I don't think
I have to say anything else thank you
Daphne
[Applause]
Thank You cassava for that very generous
introduction and for inviting me to
speak here and thank you all for being
here today so today I'm gonna tell you
about as Gustavo said my latest gig
which is in some ways related to a lot
of what the dream town challenge tries
to do so we're taking a somewhat
different tack on this idea of trying to
synthesize computational methods and
specifically machine learning with in
this case the problem of drug discovery
or drug development so so in some ways
this talk tries to put together and in
Cedro as you can tell from the name
which is really a combination of in
silicon and vitro tries to put together
these two threads of work that I think
are each incredibly influential over the
land have advanced considerably over the
last few years and I think the synthesis
is going to be incredibly powerful not
just in the context of what we're doing
but just more broadly so machine
learning specifically has advanced
incredibly just even in the last five
years so if we look back at one problem
which i think is a highlight of the
field which is the problem of image
interpretation the timeline is actually
quite striking so if you go back to you
know circa 2005 and you would show this
image to a computer and you'd ask it is
there a bear in this image a yes no
problem it would get it right about 5%
of the time fast forward seven years
after you would show this to a computer
and it might have come up with something
that says mmm wolf and now there's no
longer binary classification problem
it's a multi-class classification
problem and it's not quite right but at
least in a right general space right
fast forward a couple years beyond that
and this is a real caption for this
image it says a brown bear is swimming
in the water now this is not quite right
because the bear isn't actually swimming
but there is a bear and there is water
and furthermore this is not a one-word
label this is an actual sentence that
describes the
fast forward three years beyond that and
an actual label for this image is -
brown bear sitting on the top of rocks
that's pretty amazing and we now are at
a point where when you quantify
performance for this image recognition
problem the computer is able to do this
problem at superhuman performance
actually better than people can which I
think in the context of five years is
actually remarkable and if you'd asked
me you know 2012 when I went to Coursera
when I was going to expect this
performance I was maybe twenty years so
this is pretty remarkable what's enabled
this progress and I think this is a
really fundamental point is really the
availability of incredible amounts of
data so the training set for the image
captioning model had about 10 million
images that were derived from the web
where people sit there and describe
their images on Flickr or whatever using
these small short descriptors and that's
what's enabled that together with
simultaneously the availability of
effectively infinite amounts of compute
data using resources such as the cloud
so those together have enabled the
construction of the kinds of models that
we're talking about here
and what's really also interesting to
understand is that more data really
allows rich models to sort of come into
their own so if you look at small data
which is where we were in the image
recognition field back in the image in
that days for instance in the early days
2010 2012 we would have been there and
at that point simple models actually
outperform the more complex models but
as you increase the amount of data the
performance of the simple models
plateaus and in fact the auto climb at
the same place and the rich models
really continue to get better and better
and better as we get more data and we
haven't I think seen the ceiling on that
quite yet now what is the secret behind
the success of these models in this
context is the ability to uncover
features so the simple model
by and large were based on the fact that
people really needed to look at the raw
data and sort of extract features from
it and it turns out that even in domains
like computer vision where you would
think people kind of understand the
space because we all look at images all
the time so people would pick out
features like edges and corners and
circles that look like eyes and turns
out that people are actually not really
good at coming up with features on their
own and what these current models do
because they train end to end you get
you give them the raw data as input you
give them as hard a classification task
as output and they construct the
features in increasingly complex layers
of representation so they build up
things that are edges and then corners
and textures and then bigger things like
eyes and door handles and so on and so
forth and build up this representation
and that turns out to provide much
better performance than manually
constructed features and what's really
going on here is that this has enabled
the computer to sort of unravel a low
dimensional structure within this very
high dimensional and complex space if
you take the space of images it's
enormous right and a one hundred by a
hundred image it's actually pretty darn
small sits in the dimensional space of
256 to the power of 10,000 so that's a
pretty large space and so this is and
what the computer does is it uncovers
the low dimensional manifold in which
these images actually sit and figures
out somehow amazingly that these two
images that look nothing alike this
yellow van and this blue white thing
really sits close to each other whereas
and tractors are not quite there but
they're close and the regular car over
there the the sedan is also kind of
close even though they look nothing
alike whereas things like animals would
sit in a completely different region of
the space and those low dimensional
spaces sort of are able to uncover me
in interesting ways so here's one of my
favorite examples from a 2015 paper
which is images of men with sunglasses
sit in some region of the space images
of men without sunglasses sit in a
different region of the space there is a
distance function that's uncovered so
that if you subtract the men with the
glass with the men without the glasses
from the men with the sunglasses and add
in images of women without sunglasses
what you get in the space is women with
sunglasses which is like I don't know
mind-blowing and if you think this might
work in pixel space the answer is it
doesn't
you get garbage and you get that not
just in images you get that in other
spaces so here's another one of my
favorite examples and this is in the
space of this is what the reward Tyvek
which captures this in the context which
tries to capture semantic meaning of
words simply by using no nothing except
the context in which words are used in
very large amounts of text and it turns
out that it discovers that man is the
woman as kings the queen or walking is
to walk to swimming is to swam or Spain
is to Madrid as Germany's to Berlin all
just using raw data so that's all great
and that progress has been remarkable
and is continuing in a wide variety of
domains but it hasn't made as much of an
impact on biology quite yet and so let
me talk a little bit about the second
thread that I'd like to put together
here which is the threat of biology
biology too has made and specifically
medicine has made tremendous strides in
the last 50 years I mean starting from

our ability to drug us with great
specificity key targets that have
provided us for instance with whole new
generation of
anti-cancer drugs and
anti-inflammatories cancer immunotherapy
just in the last few years and of course
the recent Nobel Prize for that the
ability to take genetic data and really
at precision scalpel level understand
the genetic mutations that underlie
diseases such as cystic fibrosis and
provide targeted therapies for people
with very specific mutations and then as
we're now seeing the new generation of
cell therapy starting to emerge so
that's all great so awesome except not
so awesome so some of you might have
seen this graph it's a graph called
eroom's law Ihram is the inversion of
moore's law and and what you see here is
the number of drugs discovered per
billion dollars of the R&D spending
you'll notice that the graph is on the
log scale and you'll see that there is
this downward line which doesn't
correspond to a good trend of you know
the cost of computing going down
exponentially but rather the cost of
drug discovery going up exponentially or
the number of drugs per billion dollars
going down exponentially this is not a
good graph right and if you look at why
that is in 1970s it costs a few hundred
million dollars to discover a drug and
now in the 2000s it cost 2.5 billion
dollars to discover a drug now this is
not because the cost of the
investigation of any single molecule has
gone up in this ridiculous way in fact
that cost has actually gone down because
new technologies has been brought into
bear what's actually gone up is the
number of failures so what we see is and
the number of drugs if of 10,000
compounds go into step one of drug
discovery at each stage of the funnel
that number goes down and down and down
so that one compound that actually makes
it has to carry the weight of all of the
failures for which we paid those costs
and that's what's led to that 2.5
billion dollar number now if you step
back and think about this this is
actually a prediction problem that is if
we can
look at those drugs at the beginning and
say these are the ones that are more
likely to fail these are the ones that
are less likely to fail then we could
toss out the ones that are more likely
to fail and have fewer failures to carry
through the rest of the process now
we're never going to get this perfectly
because biology is really really hard
but if we could reduce that number of
failures that would hopefully help us do
better and so if this is a prediction
problem the question is machine learning
is good at prediction can we help make
this better so what we're trying to do
it in seed row is basically get at that
question so try and create a new
paradigm for drug development that uses

to design novel safe and effective
therapies that help more people faster
and the lower cost so machine learning

of ways there's a number of different
really interesting applications some of
which have been presented at this
conference I'm going to talk about this
one because it's one of my favorites
actually sorry the animation is a little

wrote what I think is the first digital

we took these a breast cancer tumor

in doing cancer grading and instead of

quantitated hundreds of different

this was manually constructed features
but it was all sorts of weird stuff that
people have never really looked at put

really simple with logistic regression
with elastic nut and and tried to

classify a predict five-year survival
much better than a competent pathologist
so that was one thing but to me which
what was far more interesting about this

that surrounds the cancer and now you

prognosis so this to me is a really nice

because I think it's a really nice one
this was 2011 in 2018 there is a bunch

you end up with odds ratios of disease
